speaker: interviewer
text: August 18, 1996. We are interviewing Enid Henson, maiden name, Jukin. The interviewer is Susie Grama. We're in Alpine, New Jersey in the United States of America, and the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: August 18, 1996. We're interviewing Enid Henson, maiden name, Jukin. The interviewer's name is Susie Grama, S-U-S-I-E G-R-A-M-A. We're in Alpine, New Jersey in the United States of America, and the language of the interview is English. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Enid what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enid Henson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-N-I-D H-E-N-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your maiden name, and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name maiden name is Jukin, U. It's J-U---

speaker: interviewer
text: K.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you born, Enid?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that? S---

speaker: interviewee
text: S.

speaker: interviewer
text: T.

speaker: interviewee
text: T.

speaker: interviewer
text: E.

speaker: interviewee
text: E.

speaker: interviewer
text: P.

speaker: interviewee
text: P.

speaker: interviewer
text: P-A-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't know if I actually [LAUGHS] know how to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The English letters are hard for you to remember. It's OK. I'll help you whenever I can. Near what large city is it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think they spell that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I check my--

speaker: interviewer
text: R-O-W-N-E. That's like on the eastern part of Poland quite near the Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote everything down, so I should have it next.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Enid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Where's my papers?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the spellings. But I-- They're all the same. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to put on my glasses. [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. You look pretty both ways. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in May 2, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your father and mother's name, please? First, tell us your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name Eli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-L-E.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jukin.

speaker: interviewer
text: J-U-K-I-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your-- do you remember when he was born? About what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's probably in the 18---

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like this. I really can't remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us your mother's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bayla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-L-E. And it's Jukin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weitzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weitzman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. W-E-I-T-Z? Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-A-N?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have brothers and/or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have the one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us his name, please and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, his name was Mendel. M-E-N---

speaker: interviewer
text: D-E-L. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-E-N-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember about when he was born? Was he older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much younger was he than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really-- I can't really tell you. But maybe by-- Mendel, what? I really don't remember. Maybe a year and a half or two years or maybe-- exactly, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good enough. That's good enough. Did you have any-- any other brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Sisters I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My--my oldest sister's was-- was Tone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that? T-O-N-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. Let me spell it to you. I'll put that down so I can-- [SIGHS] [INAUDIBLE] I don't have the right thing. I misplaced my little thing what I made.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I remember when you told me the other day. It was T-O-N-A, Tona.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tona, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember-- how much older was she than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so she was much older than you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Any other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then came Chaya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaya, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: C-H-A-Y-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was older than you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: By maybe, I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So if Tona was born 1918, how much younger was Chaya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but-- I really can't tell you exactly because--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, about. Two years younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years. Two years. Maybe younger. Maybe less. Maybe-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anybody else? Any other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then came Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-A-L-K-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And except-- I still don't know, I mean, I don't exactly how-- when she was born, what year. But around the same time-- something like two. It's hard for me to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK, anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came my brother. No, after-- after Malka, I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then came [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came my little sister, Bunia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunia?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-U-- B-U-N-E or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [SIGHS] Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: You grew up in Stepan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember. Tell me, did it have big shuls?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a big shul. We had-- all the Jewish grew up-- people lived in center of the town. Everybody knew almost everybody because it was a small area. Very nice people. That's what I think as much as I can remember. Had a lot of friends-- school friends, neighbors, things like that-- children. And my whole family lived there-- my mother's and my father's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live there for many generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the size, the general population, of all of Stepan, not only the Jewish community? Do you remember about how big it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a pretty small town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- to me, it looked big. But it was small. [LAUGHS] But there was Polish people who lived in Stepan, and Ukrainian people lived in Stepan. Some even German people lived in Stepan. And I really can't tell you how many people--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lived in-- lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the size of the Jewish community at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: The size of the Jewish community, like I said before, was the center of the city and a little bit a few streets out-- out of the center of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there more than one shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- we had a very big shul, but it had rooms like smaller-- the smaller shul and then the big sanctuary and then this. But everything in one building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had like different-- different groups praying-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the shul for the women, the shul for the men, the smaller-- the smaller minyans, you know, like I said, in that shul. And, like I said before, the big center of the-- which my father davened there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were not like orthodox like you see here with beards and things like that. But my father was orthodox. He-- he went to shul three times a day every day. He was davening by the amud, you know and they-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: That means he prayed in front of the congregation. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Congregation, all of the holidays. And is-- was-- I would say religious. We-- the whole family was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept the Sabbath and the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept the Shabbat, all the holidays. Walked to shul. There was no such a thing like taking a car of something. Strict in the house. We were not even allowed to bring a piece of paper or the-- or anything, write. We didn't go to school. But at the-- most of-- one time-- one-- there was times that we had to be in school, but I did not write. I just listened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say-- do you remember at all, were most of the Jewish people there observant in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say most, but the bigger-- bigger, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Majority?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majority was religious. That's what I think because the younger people maybe were not so. [LAUGHS] But that's what I, to my knowledge, I think that's what it [? was. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any means of Jewish education in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a Hebrew school by the name-- they called it the-- oh, forgot already. Just-- it was just on my tongue. And they-- the children-- that-- that was a private school, and you had to pay for the education. And so we had a small-- Tarbut was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tarbut. T-A-R-B-U-T?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Tarbut. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that after school or was it a full-day program.

speaker: interviewee
text: Full-day school. Full-day school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large student population there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so because I've never-- I was never-- I never went to that school so I really don't know. People-- children from richer parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did-- where did you go to school? I went to public school. And did you get a Jewish education like in the afternoon? Yeah, we had a man who lived in our house, and he was teaching us Hebrew, Jewish, how to write Jewish, how to read Hebrew, a little davening, you know. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've forgotten that. No. [LAUGHTER] I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the same-- the same teacher for a long time, or did you have different teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only remember only this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he taught all of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of my sisters. And my brother went to, I think-- was he going Cheder or not? I don't even remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there Jewish places where you could buy kosher food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a butcher store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father was a butcher, and he had a kosher butcher shop. And there were stores that you-- stores that you could buy kosher meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of homes were there? Were they little apartment buildings, or were there single family homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly single families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house was-- was considered large. It was two bedrooms, large size, big living room, nice-sized kitchen. Then we had a big garden. We had a lot. We had-- cows, chickens. We had other things. We had-- I couldn't-- it was considered as a nice size house. Also, my father's butcher shop was with the-- it's at the house, sort of like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he go to get the animals that he had to slaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he brought the animals at the outside, you know, the Gentile people were selling the animals. They were raising animals that-- for selling them. And then, we had the slaughter house, which a shochet came, and they slaughtered the-- the kosher meats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, a shochet is a slaughterer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slaughter, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did your family belong to any organizations, any Jewish or any kind of organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was a butcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside a little bit was, in the school, Polish, Ukrainian. But the main language was, in my parents' house, Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what kind of children were your friends? Were they Jewish? Were they non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find, in general, did all the Jewish children stick to each-- together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The grownup, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any-- or do you remember any types of anti-Semitism from the Polish people when you were young and growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I was too young to know that anti-Semitism is something, you know-- there's such a thing like anti-Semitism. So I really-- I know-- I knew one thing that when we were in school, everybody stick to their own-- own--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kind, yeah. We were-- we were the Jewish kids were with the Jewish kids. The Gentile kids were with the Gentile. Even the Ukrainian and the Polish didn't mix. So everybody stuck to their own thing, you know. And in class-- this was after the class-- in class, we sat together, mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that there were several nationalities in your town. Were there also Jewish people from different nationalities, or were the Jewish people all Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people from other nationalities, you know, I don't really know. But what I meant nationalities was like Polish, Ukrainian people, and the-- also, not too many families but a few German. And we used to call them the false Deutschen. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: The false Deutsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were not in their own country like-- like the-- what do we-- the-- like here, they called us the-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugees, right. Right. And the Russian, I really don't remember them. Maybe was a few but--

speaker: interviewer
text: A few what? Jewish Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish Russian? We all are Jewish Russian. [LAUGHS] Post-- yeah. No, I don't think so. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the separation wasn't only between Jew and non-Jew. It was even the other non-Jewish groups were separated according to their own kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. So that's what I-- the way I saw it. I mean, maybe it was different, but that's the way I saw it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any difference in attitude by your teachers towards Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No, everybody was like equal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't remember, like, maybe the boys fighting or somebody calling you names about being Jewish? Do you remember any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened. That happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not often, but it happened, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do, like, every day? What was your usual life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My usual life in that year was-- I can only tell you the time when [LAUGHS] I start to go to [? school. ?] We woke up in the morning, and we got dressed. We had-- we had breakfast. And we went out to school, 8 o'clock. We had to go to school. And I started school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: And before school we played with children. And I played with children in there. And then, we came home from school, and it was dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you usually come home from school? How long was the school day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school day was like probably five hours or six hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came home in the early afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, about two or three o'clock so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you go to Hebrew school afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't go to Hebrew school. I had a teacher. The tutor was in my house, my parents' house. So then I made-- had homework and did all of that whatever I ever had. And then we played a little. I played with my friends outside a little. And that's just normal like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you left Stepan when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe I was 13 years or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't working yet or anything? You were just going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any dreams or ideas you might have had of what you wanted to be when you were older?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hoped to be rich. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Because we hoped-- we were-- we were not very poor, but we were not rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that your parents were educated people at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But my father knew how to daven. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How to pray.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew how to pray and shiva. My mother was never in-- she didn't go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she never knew how to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. She didn't know how to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have, like, magazines or newspapers around in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my house. No. News-- that was luxury. That cost money. And so we used to get, like, you know, from my-- my sister, especially, from a friend or so. She-- they had a newspaper, so she took-- you know, she borrowed it, so. And every news, you know what-- what happened. We didn't have radios in the house. And we didn't have anything else. But was-- news, they always put them outside in the main streets, you know, like in the center of the city on poles like they still do it in Israel now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and announce so we knew what's going on. And some people-- I remember one family, I think, was-- was either rich, and they had the radios-- a radio. And the war broke out. Everybody went to the house to listen to the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a telephone in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what a telephone meant-- meant really-- a telephone. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the newspapers that your sister brought home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was Jewish, a Jewish paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Jewish paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, at all, when Hitler started in about 1933 when he came to power? Did it affect you in your little town at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '40, 1940, I think when they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And about September, 1939, when the Germans invaded Poland, did you hear about anything then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, but my parents did because of everybody was interested to know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that you didn't feel that the Jewish population was in danger or anything? Or do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not to my knowledge. Not me. Maybe somebody else did, but I, myself, as a child I did not feel that there was any danger until the German came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until they actually came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But up to then, I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came in, did your family make any attempts to escape or to hide their things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, they-- when they-- when they came in-- before they came in, the Russian came into our town. In 1913-- in 1939 the Russian came in. And they stayed in our-- in my area for-- and up until the German came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, like-- it was, like, a year or maybe a year and a half, we were-- well, the Russian-- and then the German came. And we-- Let's talk about the Russian occupation a little bit. You say they stayed in Stepan? The Russians were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian were-- they were the Russian government. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that-- How did that change your life? What did it do to your everyday life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what happened-- what I did to my life, [LAUGHS] I switched from public school to-- to-- to-- to a Hebrew school. Actually, it was Jewish. We were learning in Jewish. And-- and we were learning Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that because they didn't allow Jewish children in the public school anymore or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I could have gone to the public school. That's all in Russian. It was changed from Polish to Russian. But I felt as the Jewish person, I felt I wanted to be in the Jewish school. I couldn't do that before because we could not afford it. So because-- but they also learned-- we also-- it was only, not only Jewish that was allowed, we went-- we learned Russian, we were learned Ukrainian. This book-- I-- we learned a few languages that we will-- we knew, you know. It was not just like here, English. It was more than one language--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- from what I'm getting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I'm not correct. Did you feel that you wanted to be more with Jews because of the occupation-- that you felt the Jewish people had to stay together more? Is that why you switched to the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think all my friends switched, so I switched.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was just what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as you got a little older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the Russians doing in your town? What did they do there every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was-- they were running-- that was the government. And what we did, we did-- we had to go-- there wasn't enough food so we had to go and stay in line for everything that they-- that we needed-- sugar, bread. There was always lines. Always lines. It wasn't like going into the store and shop like it was before. Always-- anything-- there was every day they had something else in the store for-- not-- I'm not talking about clothing. Food. Food was very-- wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scarce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scarce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why that happened all of a sudden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I assume this was like in Russia. That's the way it was in Russia at that time too. In Russia, you always stay [NON-ENGLISH]. That means lines for everyone. I think now it's different.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Communists, yeah. The Communist party can, you know, they wanted us to convert to the party, you know, to believe it to be Communist and things like that. But nothing, nothing. We didn't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they-- what did they do? They limited the food supply? Like, what made a change all of a sudden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because all of a sudden, like, say before we find out the war has break-- was broke out, everybody around went out to shop, shop, shop because we didn't know how long it's going to last. So, and then they came in. This is what happened. We didn't have-- we had to stay in line for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have rations-- ration cards or ration coupons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it was. Maybe it was not. I don't-- I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same for the non-Jewish Poles as well as the Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any particular action against the Jews, more than against the Poles? Did they pick on any of the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened then after they were in your town? Were the Jewish people able to live as they always did, or did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a change because the-- in-- before the Russian came in and the Polish occupation was, everybody got-- had a home business. And when they came, of course, they made every business their own-- that it belonged to the-- to the Communists, to the Russians, I mean, to the government. And that's what-- that's what changed. And-- and otherwise, I don't think it changed much more than that, you know. We-- Everybody had to work the government. Not private.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there ever a ghetto established there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's when the-- the Russians were-- were-- left the-- the-- my town, Stepan, and the German came, we stayed a little-- a very short time in our own homes. And then they created the ghetto. And every--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in other words, the change of the Jewish life didn't change until the Germans arrived in your town? Do you know when that was? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, no. I think it was the-- the late '40s or early '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940, 1941? What exactly went on? Where was the ghetto located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was-- was located where the shul was, where the temple was. And they put fence all around, very high fence. And it was, like, about, I would say, about two, three small streets. All the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it included the synagogue and a few streets with Jewish-- where the Jewish people were living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the Jewish people from town and from out of town, like, some close, really just then a few families were brought in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did-- did they live in the synagogue itself also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the synagogue became-- no-- no more synagogue. They keep-- in the synagogue, I think, they were keeping the potatoes and they cooked the food there because we used to get-- we-- every day we went for-- for our, our lunch or our dinner or whatever. And they gave us soup everyday. This-- so everyone had a-- a little bit of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it for the whole day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it. And a little bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole day, that was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it? Every day. It

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a day, you went for your supplies, and you got soup and bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you, like, have it all in one meal? Or did you save it? How did it-- what did you do? What did your family do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we did, we came home. We ate it. And it was all-- the potatoes always were frozen. So we ate was never, like, sweet. When they are frozen, they are sweet, and they smelled. But for us, we didn't have anything else to eat. That's what we ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they cooked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were cooked. They cooked this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the 'they'? Who cooked it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people actually cooked them. They put the people to work. But they cooked what they had, what they gave-- what they gave them to cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to live in your own house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in the ghetto. We had to leave our house, and we went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your house was outside of the borders of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with whom did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I with my-- my mother. And they divided the ghetto in tow halves. The men went-- was-- lived in one half and the women and children in the other half side. They-- if there was a boy who became 14 years old, he had to go to the men's side. And I could only see my father-- there was also a fence between the two sides. And the only time I went to see my father, I sneaked in. Or some-- once in a while, they let you in or they let them out to see the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave a special time when people were free to walk around? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of like, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people were living where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house-- in the house I lived? I assume about 20, 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: All women and young boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And kids, children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how big was the house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house had two rooms and a kitchen. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people live in the kitchen too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kitchen ever used as a kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- There was-- there was nothing to cook unless we-- we sneaked out of the ghetto and were running going to Polish or Ukrainian people begging for some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what some people did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till they gave us some of the-- yeah. I did too sometimes. I was the only one in the family. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop this tape now. We'll continue in another minute.
